nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky and reef substrates in well-lit, high-flow zones within the photic zone

avoidWith: heavy herbivores (tangs, rabbitfish) in small tanks that may graze macroalgae

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x900. Full pinkish-purple Liagora sp. bush in clear shallow water, complete subject centered, well-lit, showing the diagnostic spindly dichotomous branching and vivid pink/purple coloration. Best overall.
Héctor Ibáñez Cantero; 2048x1536. Liagora viscida clumps with vivid pink/purple spindly branches and pale tips; sharp, strongly shows the purple colour form.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x799. Compact pinkish-white Liagora sp. clump with reddish branch tips, in-situ against rock; complete subject, shows knobby branching structure.
Nicola Crockford; 2048x1152. Single copper-pink Liagora viscida clump close-up, sharp, clearly shows the repeatedly forked spindly branching.
Héctor Ibáñez Cantero; 2048x1536. Large mounded purple-pink Liagora viscida in situ; shows the vivid purple colour form and dense branching habit.
Shaun Lee; 1024x683. Whole reddish-brown Liagora bush on open sand, complete subject well framed; different growth-habit view.
Diego Delso; 3018x3018. Sharp macro of Liagora viscida showing the fine white-and-purple-tipped dichotomous branching network in detail.
Matthieu Sontag; 1198x1600. Whitish tuft of Liagora viscida representing the pale white colour form of the genus; in-situ on reef rock.
Héctor Ibáñez Cantero; 2048x1536. Small isolated pink Liagora viscida frond cleanly showing the repeatedly forked branching against green turf.

summary: Ghastly Liagora is a specialty ornamental red macroalga with lightly calcified, spindly branching thalli ranging from white to vivid purple depending on the color form. It thrives in high-flow reef displays and is considered an intermediate-level species to keep.
careNotes:
Ghastly Liagora (Liagora sp.) is a specialty ornamental red macroalga in the family Liagoraceae, order Nemaliales — a group that encompasses dozens of species distributed across tropical and warm-temperate oceans worldwide, including the Indo-Pacific, tropical Atlantic, and Mediterranean. The genus is lightly calcified, meaning it deposits some calcium carbonate in its thallus (the algal body), though not to the degree of heavily calcareous genera such as Halimeda or Penicillus. The species is sold under two trade names representing distinct color forms: 'Ghastly Liagora' (also sold as 'Knobby Liagora') produces white-to-purple branching thalli, while 'Purple Iridescent Liagora' is a purple form with iridescent highlights along the thallus. Both share the same spindly, slippery, distinctively branching structure. Hobby-specific captive care data for Liagora is limited, as the genus has not been widely documented in general aquarium literature. Standard reef water parameters are generally advised: temperature around 24–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and a salinity of SG 1.023–1.026. High water flow is important for this species. Lighting, growth rate (slow), and nutrient uptake (low) are extrapolated from published care standards for ornamental red macroalgae in the Nemaliales and from general Liagoraceae biology. This species has been flagged as very sensitive to shipping, requiring next-day air delivery for live arrival guarantees — a sensitivity that likely carries over to aquarium acclimation and water quality stability, which supports the intermediate care rating. Propagation in the aquarium is generally accomplished by fragmentation.

Attachment: as a red alga, Ghastly Liagora will MELT at the base if buried in sand. Attach frags to rockwork using superglue, zip-tie, fishing line, or by wedging — do not place directly into a sand bed. Sand-bed placement is generally suitable only for genera such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea, plus some calcified Dasycladales.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is generally accomplished by fragmentation — detaching healthy branch segments and securing them to rockwork or rubble. Sexual reproduction, which in red algae (Rhodophyta) involves an alternating gametophyte–sporophyte life cycle, has not been documented in hobby aquaria for Liagora.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary hobby-trade source. Provides species identification, imagery, high-flow requirement note, and shipping sensitivity data. No numeric water parameters given; standard reef parameters applied from family-level extrapolation.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Purple Iridescent Liagora
additional.notes: Second Mosaic Macros listing consolidated into this entry; describes a purple iridescent color form of Liagora sp. Confirms high-flow requirement and shipping sensitivity consistent with the primary listing.

additional.name: GBIF — Liagora J.V.F.Lamouroux, 1812 (taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomic confirmation of genus placement in Liagoraceae / Nemaliales and broad distribution data (tropical and warm-temperate Atlantic, Indian, and Pacific Oceans).

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
